skill.name: security-audit
skill.description: Workflow bundle for authorized security audits of web applications, APIs, dependencies, and hardening tasks.
skill.summary: Plan and coordinate authorized security audits from reconnaissance through reporting.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution, but the reviewed file is a Markdown workflow and contains no executable script body. The Metasploit and penetration testing references are real dual-use guidance, so publication is acceptable only with authorization warnings and human review.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use penetration testing workflow
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes penetration testing commands, Metasploit, attack scenarios, and exploitation steps. This is legitimate for authorized audits, but it could aid misuse if applied to systems without permission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly lists penetration testing, Metasploit, attack scenarios, and exploitation activities. The context is an audit workflow, so the concern is dual-use risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False positive: Markdown backticks reported as shell execution
low_findings.description: The flagged backticks are Markdown code spans and fenced prompt examples naming other skills. No evidence found of Ruby or shell execution code in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown list items or prompt blocks, not executable Ruby code. The skill file contains no script interpreter context or command invocation syntax.

low_findings.title: False positive: weak cryptography keyword match
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not identify cryptographic implementation. Line 3 is descriptive metadata, and line 192 is an OWASP checklist item for insecure deserialization.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Neither location contains cryptographic code, algorithm selection, or security implementation details. The alerts appear to come from substring matching rather than semantic evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Metasploit and exploitation guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow directs users toward Metasploit and exploitation activities during the penetration testing phase. This should be restricted to authorized assessment contexts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Metasploit reference and exploitation step are explicit. The surrounding document frames them as penetration testing, which lowers confidence of malicious intent but confirms dual-use capability.

content.value_statement: Security reviews often miss steps when teams switch between reconnaissance, scanning, testing, hardening, and reporting. This skill provides a structured workflow for authorized audits across web applications, APIs, dependencies, and infrastructure.
actual_capabilities: Organizes security audits into reconnaissance, scanning, web testing, API testing, penetration testing, hardening, and reporting phases.
Lists related skills to invoke for OWASP testing, dependency scanning, SAST, API fuzzing, and hardening.
Provides copy-paste prompts for coordinating focused security testing tasks.
Includes an OWASP Top 10 checklist and API security checklist.
Defines quality gates for documenting vulnerabilities, risk assessments, remediation steps, and reports.
limitations: It is a workflow guide and does not run scanners or validate findings by itself.
It references dual-use penetration testing tools that require explicit authorization.
It depends on separate skills and tools that may not be installed.
It does not replace legal review, threat modeling, or expert manual testing.

use_cases.title: Prepare an application security review
use_cases.description: Create a phased audit plan for a web application before a release or compliance review.

use_cases.title: Structure API security testing
use_cases.description: Coordinate endpoint enumeration, authentication checks, rate limit testing, input validation, and reporting.

use_cases.title: Track remediation readiness
use_cases.description: Use the quality gates to confirm that findings, impact, and remediation steps are documented.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic audit plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for an authorized assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this security-audit workflow to create a phased audit plan for my authorized web application review. Include scope, phases, and deliverables.

prompt_templates.title: Review API security coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this workflow to plan API security testing for an authorized system. Cover authentication, authorization, rate limits, validation, errors, and reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Map findings to remediation work
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this workflow to turn confirmed security findings into remediation tasks. Include severity, affected area, owner, verification step, and reporting status.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate a full assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this workflow to coordinate a full authorized security assessment across reconnaissance, scanning, web testing, API testing, hardening, and final reporting.

output_examples.input: Plan an audit for a customer-facing web application.
output_examples.output: A phased audit plan with reconnaissance, vulnerability scanning, OWASP testing, API checks, hardening tasks, and final report deliverables.

output_examples.input: Help prepare remediation tracking after a security review.
output_examples.output: A prioritized remediation tracker covering vulnerability description, risk level, owner, due date, verification method, and report status.

output_examples.input: Check whether an API review has enough coverage.
output_examples.output: A coverage checklist for authentication, authorization, rate limits, validation, error handling, headers, and documentation gaps.

best_practices: Use this workflow only for systems where testing is explicitly authorized.
Define scope, allowed tools, test windows, and reporting contacts before starting.
Validate automated findings manually before assigning severity or remediation work.
anti_patterns: Do not use penetration testing prompts against systems without written permission.
Do not treat scanner output as confirmed vulnerability evidence without review.
Do not publish exploit details beyond what the remediation audience needs.

faq.question: Does this skill run security scanners?
faq.answer: No. It organizes an audit workflow and points users toward related skills and tools.

faq.question: Is this safe for production systems?
faq.answer: It can support production reviews only with approval, defined scope, and safe test windows.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this workflow?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include penetration testing guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It references penetration testing phases and Metasploit, so authorization controls are required.

faq.question: What outputs should users expect?
faq.answer: Users should expect plans, checklists, remediation tracking, and report structure rather than executed tests.

faq.question: Can this replace a security expert?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure work, but expert review is needed for scope, legality, exploit safety, and final risk decisions.
